rationale: The citing paper uses Dhar and Wertenbroch (2000) as core support for its hedonic-versus-utilitarian product distinction, not just as a peripheral citation. That distinction is then built into hypothesis development (H2) and directly operationalized in Experiment 2 to test moderation of simulation effects by product type. Although the paper does not test Dhar and Wertenbroch's original acquisition-versus-forfeiture mechanism, it relies on their conceptualization of hedonic goods as experiential and utilitarian goods as functional to structure its own theory and design.
relation_description: The citing paper applies the hedonic-versus-utilitarian framework from Dhar and Wertenbroch (2000) to a different question: how process versus outcome simulation works under cognitive versus affective modes. It uses that framework to motivate and test a product-type moderator in its experiments.
